the culpability of a health practitioner  on the basis
of the civil standard of
proof, bearing in mind that serious allegations require a high level of proof.

summary.brief:
7         New Zealand authorities currently require the Tribunal to assess
the culpability of a health practitioner  on the basis
of the civil standard of
proof, bearing in mind that serious allegations require a high level of proof. 
InBrake v Preliminary Proceedings Committee1a full Court  of the High  Court  expressed  the standard  of proof 
in the following way:. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: On 24 November Ms Wilson and her partner attended a consultation with Dr Johri regarding a lump in her left breast.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal is satisfied that Dr Johri’s acts and omissions were a serious departure from the standards ordinarily expected of a general practitioner.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal has determined Dr Johri’s conduct constitutes professional misconduct and that he should be censured and ordered to pay 30% of the costs.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Director of Proceedings alleged that the conduct set out in all three particulars amounted to professional misconduct when viewed cumulatively.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Following a mammography and fine needle cytology a diagnosis of left breast carcinoma was confirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal believes that the interests of society and the profession are best served by way of a censure.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Ms Wilson was induced and gave birth by way of caesarean section to a baby girl.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal is of the view that much of the jurisprudence concerning the meaning of professional misconduct under earlier legislative regimes continues to be relevant under the Act.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: The midwife’s notes documented Ms Wilson had a lump on her left breast and that she had advised Ms Wilson to see her general practitioner.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal is satisfied that in the circumstances of this case a disciplinary finding is needed in order to protect the public and/or maintain professional standards.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient reports a three-day history of a painful lump in the left breast.

focused_exam.breast: 3cm lump in left breast, considered likely a blocked duct with infection or a tumor

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Breast lump - differential includes blocked duct with infection or tumor

requestables.name: Mammography
requestables.description: Breast imaging to evaluate the lump

reveal.result_summary: Diagnosis of left breast carcinoma confirmed
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt urgent referral and treatment

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with a breast lump

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with a breast lump during pregnancy
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Refer to a breast specialist immediately
options.clinical_reasoning: Urgent referral needed due to potential malignancy

options.description: Wait and see if the lump resolves with antibiotics
options.clinical_reasoning: Inappropriate delay in potential cancer diagnosis

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant chose to wait and see
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed lump might resolve with antibiotics

expected_action_court.description: Immediate referral to a specialist was necessary
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established urgency due to potential malignancy

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in referral led to progression of cancer
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Immediate specialist referral and further diagnostic workup
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Negligence in failing to act on potential malignancy

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed advanced breast cancer and died

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to refer to a specialist
Causation established - delay contributed to progression of cancer

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to refer to a specialist
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Referral could have led to earlier diagnosis and treatment

factual_timeline.event: Patient consults Dr Johri about breast lump

allegations.allegation: Failure to refer to a specialist

court_findings.finding: Dr Johri failed to adhere to expected standards
